The matrix has τ = –1 and Δ = –6, so the characteristic equation is λ 2 + λ – 6 = 0. Hence λ 1 = 2 , λ 2 = − 3 Next we find the eigenvectors. Given an eigenvalue λ, the corresponding eigenvector v = (v 1 , v 2 ) satisfies ( 1 − λ 1 4 − 2 − λ ) ( v 1 v 2 ) = ( 0 0 ) . For λ 1 ,=2, this yield ( − 1 1 4 − 1 ) ( v 1 v 2 ) = ( 0 0 ) , which has a nontrivial solution (v 1 , v 2 )=(1, 1), or any scalar multiple thereof. (Of course, any multiple of an eigenvector is always an eigenvector; we try to pick the simplest multiple, but any one will do.) Similarly, for λ 2 = –3, the eigenvector equation becomes ( 4 1 4 1 ) ( v 1 v 2 ) = ( 0 0 ) , which has a nontrivial solution (v 1 , v 2 ) = (1,–4). In summary, v 1 = ( 1 1 ) , v 2 = ( 1 − 4 ) . Next we write the general solution as a linear combination of eigensolutions. From (6), the general solution is x ( t ) = c 1 ( 1 1 ) e 2 t + c 1 ( 1 − 4 ) e − 3 t . (7) Finally, we compute c 1 and c 2 to satisfy the initial condition ( x 0 , y 0 ) = (2, –3). At t = 0, (7) becomes ( 2 − 3 ) = c 1 ( 1 1 ) + c 2 ( 1 − 4 ) which is equivalent to the algebraic system 2 = c 1 + c 2 , − 3 = c 2 − 4 c 2 . The solution is c 1 = 1, c 2 = 1. Substituting back into (7) yields x ( t ) = e 2 t + e − 3 t , y ( t ) = e 2 t − 4 e − 3 t for the solution to the initial value problem. ■ Whew! Fortunately we don’t need to go through all this to draw the phase portrait of a linear system. All we need to know are the eigenvectors and eigenvalues. EXAMPLE 5.2.2: Draw the phase portrait for the system of Example 5.2.1. Solution: The system has eigenvalues λ 1 = 2, λ 2 = –3. Hence the first eigensolution grows exponentially, and the second eigensolution decays. This means the origin is a saddle point. Its stable manifold is the line spanned by the eigenvector v 2 = (1, –4), corresponding to the decaying eigensolution. Similarly, the unstable manifold is the line spanned by v 1 = (1, 1). As with all saddle points, a typical trajectory approaches the unstable manifold as t → ∞, and the stable manifold as t → –∞. Figure 5.2.2 shows the phase portrait. ■ Figure 5.2.2 EXAMPLE 5.2.3: Sketch a typical phase portrait for the case λ 2 < λ 1 < 0. Solution: First suppose λ 2 < λ 1 < 0. Then both eigensolutions decay exponentially. The fixed point is a stable node, as in Figures 5.1.5a and 5.1.5c , except now the eigenvectors are not mutually perpendicular, in general. Trajectories typically approach the origin tangent to the slow eigendirection , defined as the direction spanned by the eigenvector with the smaller |λ|. In backwards time ( t → –∞), the trajectories become parallel to the fast eigendirection. Figure 5.2.3 shows the phase portrait. (If we reverse all the arrows in Figure 5.2.3 , we obtain a typical phase portrait for an unstable node.) ■ Figure 5.2.3 EXAMPLE 5.2.4: What happens if the eigenvalues are complex numbers? Solution: If the eigenvalues are complex, the fixed point is either a center ( Figure 5.2.4a ) or a spiral ( Figure 5.2.4b ). We’ve already seen an example of a center in the simple harmonic oscillator of Section 5.1 ; the origin is surrounded by a family of closed orbits. Note that centers are neutrally stable , since nearby trajectories are neither attracted to nor repelled from the fixed point. A spiral would occur if the harmonic oscillator were lightly damped. Then the trajectory would just fail to close, because the oscillator loses a bit of energy on each cycle. Figure 5.2.4 To justify these statements, recall that the eigenvalues are λ 1 , 2 = 1 2 ( τ ± τ 2 − 4 Δ ) . Thus complex eigenvalues occur when τ 2 − 4 Δ < 0. To simplify the notation, let’s write the eigenvalues as λ 1 , 2 = α ± i ω where α = τ / 2 , ω = 1 2 4 Δ − τ 2 . By assumption, ω ≠ 0. Then the eigenvalues are distinct and so the general solution is still given by x ( t ) = c 1 e λ 1 t v 1 + c 2 e λ 2 t v 2 . But now the c ’s and v ’s are complex , since the λ’s are. This means that x( t ) involves linear combinations of e ( a±iω)t . By Euler’s formula, e i ω t = cos ω t + i sin ωt. Hence x( t ) is a combination of terms involving e α t cos ut and e α t sin ωt. Such terms represent exponentially decaying oscillations if α = Re( λ ) < 0 and growing oscillations if α > 0. The corresponding fixed points are stable and unstable spirals
